vite_cons: No built-in API routes (need separate backend)
why_not_vite.decision: Next.js chosen because API routes allow us to keep backend logic in the same repo, SSR helps with game landing page SEO, and PWA support is first-class.

react_native_cons: No web version without extra work
why_not_react_native.decision: PWA approach - one codebase, instant updates, no app store gatekeeping. Can wrap in Capacitor later for native stores if needed.

godot_unity_cons: Harder to integrate with modern web stack
why_not_godot_unity.decision: Web-first HTML5/Canvas approach - lighter, faster loading, easier to iterate, better integration with web services.

database_design_principles.normalization: 3NF for relational data (players, accounts), denormalized JSONB for game config (items, quests)
database_design_principles.partitioning: Consider time-based partitioning for battle_logs, event_logs when >1M rows
database_design_principles.indexing_strategy: B-tree on primary keys, GIN on JSONB columns, BRIN on timestamps
database_design_principles.soft_deletes: All tables have deleted_at column - never hard delete player data

client_side.localStorage: User preferences, auth tokens, offline action queue
client_side.IndexedDB: Game assets cache, large config files
client_side.Cache API: Service worker cache for static assets

server_side.redis: Session data, rate limits, leaderboard hot data
server_side.cdn: Game assets, static images, audio files
server_side.db_cache: Materialized views for expensive queries (leaderboard aggregates)

realtime_architecture.fallback: Short polling every 30s for clients without WebSocket support
realtime_architecture.bandwidth_optimization: Delta updates only, full sync on reconnect

src_structure.lib/: Utility functions, game logic, API clients
